# WoofScore

> WoofScore is a free web tool that grades any dog food's ingredient list A–F. Paste the ingredients off the bag and it scores overall quality, flags controversial ingredients (artificial colours, BHA/BHT, menadione, unnamed by-product meal, cheap fillers, added sugar) and highlights the good ones (named animal proteins, whole foods, beneficial supplements). Scoring criteria are built on AAFCO ingredient definitions, FDA pet-food guidance and veterinary-nutrition references. It is informational, not veterinary advice.

## What it answers
- "Is this dog food good / high quality?" — paste the ingredients for an instant A–F WoofScore.
- "Which dog food ingredients are bad / controversial?" — each flagged ingredient is explained on its own page.
- "Can dogs eat [human food]?" — clearly-sourced safe / toxic / moderation answers with serving guidance.

## How the grade works
Named animal proteins (chicken, beef, salmon, named meat meals), whole grains/veg/fruit, and beneficial supplements raise the score; vague by-products, cheap fillers (corn gluten, wheat middlings, cellulose), artificial colours and dyes, sugar, and controversial preservatives (BHA, BHT, ethoxyquin) lower it. Ingredient order (concentration) is weighted, and any "avoid"-tier ingredient caps the grade. Output is an A–F grade plus per-ingredient flags.

## Sources
Criteria draw on AAFCO ingredient definitions (aafco.org), FDA pet-food guidance (fda.gov), and the ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center (aspca.org) for toxic-food classifications.

## Key pages
- Home / dog food ingredient checker: https://woofscore.com/
- Ingredient database (good/bad/controversial): https://woofscore.com/dog-food-ingredients.html
- Can dogs eat…? (safe & toxic foods): https://woofscore.com/can-dogs-eat.html
- Scoring methodology: https://woofscore.com/methodology.html
- About & independence: https://woofscore.com/about.html

## About
WoofScore is an independent, free informational tool for dog owners. Grades are an ingredient-level analysis against published AAFCO and FDA criteria, not a substitute for veterinary advice. Always consult your veterinarian about your dog's diet.
